preFix.title: Repairing a chipped stair tread nosing
preFix.subtitle: Fill and re-finish a damaged stair tread edge
preFix.excerpt: How to repair a chipped or broken stair tread nosing using two-part wood filler or epoxy wood repair compound, including preparation, shaping the filler to match the nosing profile, sanding smooth, and finishing to match the existing tread.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Ronseal Two Part Wood Filler technical data. Osmo Hardwax Oil application guide.

content.text: is both a trip hazard and an eyesore. On a softwood staircase, it is also common: pine nosings wear quickly at the edge and split with heavy foot traffic. Standard one-part filler is too soft for this repair; it will crumble within weeks. A
sets hard enough to take foot traffic and sands to a smooth profile that matches the nosing. This is a beginner job in technique but requires patience in shaping the cured filler.

Remove any loose splinters from the chip with the utility knife. Undercut the edges of the chip slightly: a small V-cut around the perimeter: to give the filler something to key into. Brush out all dust and debris. If the tread is painted or varnished, sand the area around the chip back to bare wood for 20 mm in all directions; the filler will not bond to a glossy surface.

Mix the base and hardener on a piece of card or tile according to the pack ratio: usually a pea-sized amount of hardener to a golf-ball-sized amount of base. Mix thoroughly until the colour is uniform: typically a light cream. Do not mix more than you can use in five to ten minutes as the filler will begin to cure in the pot. In warm conditions, it sets faster.

Press the filler firmly into the chip with the 50 mm filling knife, working it into the undercut edges. Build the filler slightly proud of the surrounding surface and the nosing profile: you will sand it back to the correct shape once cured. On a deep chip, apply in two layers: the first to fill the bulk and create a key, the second after the first has cured (15 to 20 minutes) to build up to slightly proud. One thick layer applied without an intermediate cure tends to slump as it sets.
Step 4, shape and sand the cured filler

Once the filler is fully hard (30 to 45 minutes at room temperature), sand back the proud area with 80-grit sandpaper wrapped around the sanding block. Work along the length of the nosing to shape the curve. Check frequently against the surrounding nosing profile. Progress through 120-grit and then 180-grit to refine the surface. The final surface should be smooth to the touch and indistinguishable in profile from the undamaged nosing on either side.

Two-part filler is not absorbent and will not take a stain the way the surrounding wood does. On a stained or oiled tread, the repair must be primed with a shellac or primer sealer before the finish coat: otherwise the topcoat slides off the filler. On a painted tread, prime and paint in the usual way. On a varnished or oiled tread, the filler patch will be slightly visible in raking light at the colour boundary; there is no way to avoid this with a patch repair. If the nosing is very badly damaged, replacement is a cleaner solution.

On a hardwood nosing (oak, ash) where colour match matters, two-part filler comes in various shades: mid oak, light oak, mahogany, pine. Mix shades to match the timber before applying. Epoxy repair compounds are harder than standard two-part filler and better suited to a busy nosing. They file and sand cleanly and take finish equally well.

attrs.fix: Remove the soft filler, clean the area, and reapply correctly mixed two-part filler. Always mix until the colour is fully uniform — streaks indicate unhardened base.
attrs.cause: One-part filler used instead of two-part; two-part not mixed to the correct ratio.
attrs.symptom: Filler crumbles or chips out after a few weeks

attrs.fix: Scrape off the stiffening filler and start again with a smaller mix quantity. Work faster. In hot weather, work in the morning when the room is cooler.
attrs.cause: Too much hardener mixed in; room temperature very warm; mixed too far in advance.
attrs.symptom: Filler cured before it could be applied to the chip

attrs.fix: Sand the filler area and apply a shellac primer before refinishing. If using a tinted finish, test the colour on an offcut first.
attrs.cause: Filler colour does not match the timber; stain or oil applied directly to filler without priming.
attrs.symptom: Colour is visibly different from the surrounding tread after finishing

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
